content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance · · Administration · · Executive/Legal · · Enforcement · · Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        323-5660            322-5901           322-4441            322-5444

March 22, 1984


Arthur Forcier
11783 Laurelcrest Drive
Studio City, CA 91604

                                        Re: Your Request for Advice
                                            Our No. A-84-043

Dear Mr. Forcier:

     I received the additional materials you sent with reference
to your advice request. You are a consultant to Carson League
of Voters, a nonpartisan citizens group in the City of Carson
which wishes to prepare and mail home ballot applications to
voters in Carson and to otherwise encourage voter participation
in the electoral process. You provided me with drafts of these
materials. You asked whether, by virtue of these activities,
this group would have any reporting requirements under the
Political Reform Act.1/ You also asked whether the group
would have to identify itself as the sender on its mailings
under Section 84305.

     The campaign disclosure provisions of the Act require that
all candidates and committees shall file campaign statements at
specified times and places. Sections 84200, et seq. A
committee is "any person or combination of persons who directly:

     (a) Receives contributions totaling five hundred
dollars ($500) or more in a calendar year;

     (b) Makes independent expenditures totaling five
hundred dollars ($500) or more in a calendar year; or

     (c) Makes contributions totaling five thousand
dollars ($5,000) or more in a calendar year to or at
the behest of candidates or committees.

                    (Section 82013.)

_____________________

1/   Government Code Sections 81000-91014; all statutory
references are to the Government Code.

Arthur Forcier
March 22, 1984
Page 2


     The Act defines contributions and expenditures to include
all payments unless it is clear from the surrounding
circumstances that they are not made for political purposes.
Sections 82015 and 82025.

     The Commission has elaborated on the meaning of "political
purpose" in its regulations. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Sections
18215 and 18225 (copies enclosed). These sections provide that
political purposes means "made for the purpose of influencing or
attempting to influence the action of the voters for or against
the nomination or election of a candidate or candidates or the
qualification or passage of any measure."

     All payments received by an organization which is formed
primarily for political purposes for which full and adequate
consideration is not exchanged are contributions. Section
82015; 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215(b)(4). Likewise all
payments made by an organization formed primarily for political
purposes are expenditures within the meaning of the Act.
Section 82025; 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18225(b)(4).

     From your description of the Carson League of Voters and a
review of the materials it appears that it is not formed
primarily for or against a candidate or candidates or the
passage of any measure. Its primary goal is to increase voter
turnout in Carson's municipal elections. Therefore, monies
received, or spent, by the group do not automatically constitute
contributions or expenditures under 2 Cal. Adm. Code Sections
18215 and 18225. Unless the group undertakes some other
additional activities or expenditures which make it a committee,
it need not file campaign reports or a Statement of Organization
as a recipient committee with the Secretary of State pursuant to
Section 84101.2/ See Opinion requested by Francis Wilmarth,
2 FPPC Opinions 130 (No. 75-188, August 3, 1976) (copy
enclosed). If this occurs, please feel free to contact this
office for additional advice.

     In addition, since the group is not a "committee" based upon
its present activities, it is not required to identify itself on
mass mailings. See Commission regulation, 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18435 (copy enclosed).

_______________________

     2/ Only recipient committees, or committees as defined in
Section 82013(a), are required to file Statements of
Organization.

Arthur Forcier
March 22, 1984
Page 3


     I hope the foregoing discussion answers all of your
questions.  If I can be of further assistance, please feel free
to contact me.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Diane Maura Fishburn
                                        Staff Counsel
                                        Legal Division

DMF:plh
Enclosures

2/25 1984

TO: FPPC FROM: A. Forcefer [illegible]
11783 Laurelcrest Drive,
StuddCity, 2A-3 [illegible] 94

Are the costs of the production & distribution
of materials such as the enclosed "A","B", "C",
"D" & "E" reportable?

Does the expenditure of over $10,000 for such
materials force one to become a committee or
report anything to anyone? Can one raise money
from various individuals or organizations & use
it to produce & distribute such materials? Does
that change the reporting or committee creation
aspects? Does it make a difference if the material
goes to people of more than one political party?
Does it make a difference if it doesn't name any
candidate or ballot measure? Do such materials
have to be identified as to sponsors, or purchasers,
or producers etc?

Do the enclosed materials influence or attempt
to influence the action of the voters for or
against the nomination or election of a candidate
or the passage or defeat of any measure or quali-
fication of a petition?

If over 200 of the enclosed materials are:mailed
(200 each or 200 or any particular one), does the
sender have to be identified?

The 1982 FPPC manual is very vague. Three times
during 1983 I asked for a new manual. I never
received one. Please send me the latest manual &
the answers to the questions in this note. I need
the answers in relation to the enclosed materials.
In one sense everything everyone does or doesn't
do helps and hurts all candidates & causes. Where
does the FPPC draw the line & what is the line?

On Tuesday June 2nd, the City of Carson is holding a Special
Election to fill a vacancy on the City Council. We urge you to
participate in that election.

The last time our city held a special election, the leading can-
didate was the choice of 1463 voters, less than two percent of
the total population. It is up to all of us to do better this time.

You can help to keep our community strong by voting on
Tuesday June 2nd and by putting this sign in your window as
a voting reminder to your neighbors.

After you vote, you are invited to enjoy dinner at a discount at
any of the restaurants listed below. Just take your ballot stub
and the coupon on this card to any of these fine restaurants
and you can enjoy a good meal at a bargain price.

By voting on Tuesday June 2nd, you will be deciding the
future of our city. Don't leave that future in the hands of
others.

OFFER GOOD AT ANY OF THESE RESTAURANTS
TUESDAY JUNE 2, 1981 UNTIL CLOSING TIME

JANOS PIZZA
139 E. Lomita Blvd.
$2.00 Off the Cost of Any Pizza

ANNIE Z's
Corner Carson & Figueroa
50¢ Off on Any Food Order

LAS PALMAS CAFFE
23909 So. Avalon Blvd.
$3.00 Off on Any Dinner Order

FLO'S BROASTED CHICKEN
2503 E. Carson Street
Free Bowl of Chicken When You Buy 1 Pc. or More

KENTUCKY FRIED CHICKEN
543 E. Sepulveda Blvd.
$1.00 Off on Bucket or Barrel of Chicken

LUPITAS MEXICAN RESTAURANT
21917 So. Main Street
$3.00 Off on Any Dinner Order

To qualify for discount you must bring your ballot stub from
he June 2, 1981 Carson City Election and the coupon from
his sign at the time of purchase. One discount per stub. Offer
food until closing time on June 2, 1981 only.

I'M GOING TO

HAVE

ON TUESDAY

SUPPLIES

[illegible] R ill ( ) TH's
[illegible] P HATE
[illegible] T ORE 2 NEFTS
[illegible] R IGHT

VOTE
TUESDAY

APRIL 13

Home Ballot Application • Mail by May 31, 1965

sections.question: s.
sections.conclusion: From your description of the Carson League of Voters and a review of the materials it appears that it is not formed primarily for or against a candidate or candidates or the passage of any measure. Its primary goal is to increase voter turnout in Carson's municipal elections. Therefore, monies received, or spent, by the group do not automatically constitute contributions or expenditures under 2 cal. Adm. Code Sections 18215 and 18225. Unless the group undertakes some other additional activities or expenditures which make it a committee, it need not file campaign reports or a Statement of Organization as a recipient committee with the Secretary of State pursuant to Section 84101. In addition, since the group is not a "committee" based upon its present activities, it is not required to identify itself on mass mailings.
sections.question_synthetic: Does the Carson League of Voters, a nonpartisan group promoting voter participation through ballot applications and voter outreach, have campaign reporting requirements and must it identify itself on mass mailings?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Carson League of Voters is not a committee under FPPC regulations because its primary purpose is increasing voter turnout rather than supporting or opposing candidates or measures, and therefore it has no reporting requirements and is not required to identify itself on mass mailings.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Clear FPPC response letter with explicit Question and Conclusion sections. Contains supporting materials including incoming request from Forcier and sample voter outreach materials. OCR quality is good for main letter content.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: s.

CONCLUSION: From your description of the Carson League of Voters and a review of the materials it appears that it is not formed primarily for or against a candidate or candidates or the passage of any measure. Its primary goal is to increase voter turnout in Carson's municipal elections. Therefore, monies received, or spent, by the group do not automatically constitute contributions or expenditures under 2 cal. Adm. Code Sections 18215 and 18225. Unless the group undertakes some other additional activities or expenditures which make it a committee, it need not file campaign reports or a Statement of Organization as a recipient committee with the Secretary of State pursuant to Section 84101. In addition, since the group is not a "committee" based upon its present activities, it is not required to identify itself on mass mailings.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises Arthur Forcier that the Carson League of Voters, a nonpartisan voter participation group, is not a committee under the Political Reform Act and therefore has no campaign reporting requirements or mass mailing identification obligations based on its activities of distributing ballot applications and encouraging voter turnout.
